icd_category: N02.8 — Recurrent and persistent hematuria with other morphologic changes
one_liner: A 26-year-old male presents with episodic gross hematuria coinciding with upper respiratory infections and is found to have persistent microscopic hematuria and proteinuria.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient reports 3 episodes of cola-colored urine over the past 18 months, each occurring 1-2 days after the onset of a sore throat or upper respiratory infection and lasting 2-3 days before resolving. Between episodes, he has had no visible hematuria. He presented today because his PCP found persistent microscopic hematuria and proteinuria on a routine urinalysis performed 2 weeks after his most recent episode. He denies dysuria, flank pain, joint pain, skin rash, or recent travel. No history of hearing loss.
stage2.family_history: Father has hypertension. No family history of kidney disease or hearing loss.
stage2.social_history: Graduate student. Non-smoker, occasional social alcohol. No illicit drugs.
stage2.physical_exam: Well-appearing young male in no acute distress. Pharynx mildly erythematous without exudate. No periorbital or peripheral edema. Heart: regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs: clear bilaterally. Abdomen: soft, non-tender, no masses or organomegaly; No rash, purpura, or joint swelling; No hearing deficit on bedside testing.

lab_results.result: pH 5.5, 2+ blood, 1+ protein, 20-50 RBCs/hpf (many dysmorphic), RBC casts present, no WBC casts
lab_results.clinical_significance: Dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts indicate glomerular origin of hematuria
Subnephrotic proteinuria; >1 g/day is an adverse prognostic factor in this condition
Mildly elevated for a 26-year-old male; early renal impairment
Mildly reduced; concerning in a young patient
Elevated in ~50% of this condition patients; supportive but not diagnostic
Normal complement is expected in this condition; helps exclude lupus nephritis and MPGN

imaging_results.result: Normal-sized kidneys bilaterally (right 11.5 cm, left 11.8 cm) with normal cortical echogenicity. No hydronephrosis, cysts, or masses.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Normal renal ultrasound; structural abnormalities excluded

imaging_results.result: Light microscopy: mesangial hypercellularity and matrix expansion. Immunofluorescence: dominant mesangial IgA deposits with co-deposition of C3 and lesser IgG. Electron microscopy: electron-dense deposits in the mesangium. Oxford classification: M1 E0 S0 T0 C0.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Mesangial IgA-dominant deposits are diagnostic of the suspected condition; Oxford/MEST-C score guides prognosis

key_tests: Urinalysis with microscopy (dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts)
acceptable_tests: ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM antibodies (to exclude other causes)
low_yield_tests: Anti-PLA2R antibody (for membranous nephropathy, not IgA)

stage5.final_clue: Synpharyngitic gross hematuria (occurring during rather than after URI), persistent microscopic hematuria with dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts, subnephrotic proteinuria, and renal biopsy showing.

debrief.classic_presentation: A young adult male presenting with episodic gross hematuria that occurs concurrently with (synpharyngitic) upper respiratory infections, persistent microscopic hematuria between episodes, mild proteinuria, and mild hypertension. Biopsy shows mesangial IgA deposits.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: IgA nephropathy is distinguished from post-streptococcal GN by the timing of hematuria: IgA nephropathy causes synpharyngitic hematuria (during the URI, latency 1-2 days), while PSGN has a latent period of 1-3 weeks after pharyngitis. The Oxford MEST-C classification (Mesangial hypercellularity, Endocapillary proliferation, Segmental sclerosis, Tubular atrophy/interstitial fibrosis, Crescents) independently predicts renal outcomes. The TESTING trial and STOP-IGA trial provide conflicting evidence on immunosuppression. Recent data supports targeted-release budesonide (Nefecon/Tarpeyo) for IgA nephropathy with proteinuria >1 g/day despite optimized supportive care.
debrief.pathophysiology: IgA nephropathy results from the deposition of galactose-deficient IgA1 (Gd-IgA1) immune complexes in the glomerular mesangium. The pathogenesis involves a multi-hit model: (1) increased production of Gd-IgA1, (2) formation of autoantibodies against Gd-IgA1, (3) formation of immune complexes, (4) mesangial deposition triggering complement activation, mesangial proliferation, and inflammatory injury. Mucosal infections (particularly pharyngeal) trigger surges in IgA production, causing episodic gross hematuria. Approximately 30-40% of patients progress to ESRD over 20-30 years.
debrief.treatment_overview: Supportive care first: ACE inhibitor or ARB titrated to maximally tolerated dose to reduce proteinuria (target <1 g/day). SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin) based on DAPA-CKD trial data. Blood pressure goal <130/80 mmHg. If proteinuria remains >1 g/day after 3-6 months of optimized supportive care, consider targeted-release budesonide (Tarpeyo/Nefecon) or systemic corticosteroids. Immunosuppressive therapy (mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide) is considered for rapidly progressive disease or crescentic IgA nephropathy. Fish oil (omega-3 fatty acids) has modest evidence. Tonsillectomy has been studied in Japan but is not standard practice globally.
high_yield_pearls: IgA nephropathy is the most common primary glomerulonephritis worldwide; diagnosis requires renal biopsy showing dominant mesangial IgA deposits on immunofluorescence.
Synpharyngitic hematuria (during URI, latency 1-2 days) distinguishes IgA nephropathy from post-streptococcal GN (latency 1-3 weeks after pharyngitis).
Key prognostic factors for poor outcome: proteinuria >1 g/day, reduced GFR at presentation, hypertension, and Oxford MEST-C score (S1, T1-2 are particularly adverse).
IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schonlein purpura) is the systemic form of IgA deposition disease; renal biopsy findings are identical to IgA nephropathy.
Serum IgA levels are elevated in ~50% of patients but are neither sensitive nor specific for diagnosis; biopsy is always required.
sources: Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) 2021 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Glomerular Diseases — https://kdigo.org/guidelines/

Lv J et al. Effect of oral methylprednisolone on clinical outcomes in patients with IgA nephropathy (TESTING). JAMA. 2017;318(5):432-442. — https://jamanetwork.com/searchresults?SearchTerm=Lv+J+et+al.+Effect+of+oral+methylprednisolone+on+clinical+outcomes+in+patients+with+IgA+nephropathy+%28TESTING%29.+JAMA.+2017%3B318%285%29%3A432-442.
UpToDate: IgA nephropathy — treatment and prognosis (2024) — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search

after_stage2_more_likely: IgA nephropathy — young male with synpharyngitic gross hematuria, persistent microscopic hematuria, mild proteinuria, mild hypertension
Thin basement membrane disease — episodic hematuria in a young patient, but typically without proteinuria or hypertension
after_stage2_less_likely: Post-streptococcal GN — hematuria occurs during (not weeks after) the URI; no edema or hypocomplementemia expected
Alport syndrome — no hearing loss, no family history of renal disease or deafness
after_labs_top3: IgA nephropathy — confirmed by biopsy showing dominant mesangial IgA deposits; elevated serum IgA, normal complement, RBC casts
IgA vasculitis (HSP nephritis) — identical biopsy findings but no purpura, abdominal pain, or arthralgia to suggest systemic disease
Post-streptococcal GN — normal ASO titer and normal complement levels make this unlikely
